artifacts.content:
Hi Elena,

I’m on one of the unit computers during “free time,” so I’ll try to keep this semi-short, but my brain is all over the place.

They admitted me here at the Providence Center for Mental Health – Adult Inpatient Unit on 2/24, so I’m a couple days in now. My attending is Dr. Sarah K. Whitman, MD – she’s the psychiatrist running my care – and they keep mentioning coordinating with Michael Lawson and my PCP, Dr. Emily J. Carter. It’s weird hearing all these people who’ve just been names on MyChart suddenly talking to each other about me.

I keep thinking how this all built up. Remember how back on 2/21 at the Gresham apartment I went out to the garage around 11:30 p.m., after basically only 3 hours of sleep in two nights, and downed half that cheap vodka and six Tylenol PM on top of the 150 mg sertraline? I woke up on the floor and still tried to pretend it “didn’t really count.” I only told you the next morning. Sitting here now, it’s pretty obvious that was a huge red flag.

And then Monday 2/23, when I drove out to the I‑84 overpass near the 102nd exit around 7 a.m. and sat there for almost 40 minutes trying to work up the nerve to go over the edge… I swear the only thing that pulled me back was Sofia texting to ask if I was still coming to her conference at Lincoln Elementary that night.

The staff keep reminding me this *is* life‑or‑death, but part of me still wants to say, “I know it’s not *that* serious, I can probably manage at home.” I’m glad you pushed me, even when I was minimizing everything.

How are the kids doing with homework and inhalers and all that? Please tell Sofia I’m proud of her and tell Mateo I miss his dinosaur facts.

Love you,
Laura

Hi Michael,

I wanted to fill you in a bit from my side while I’m here at Providence Center for Mental Health – Adult Inpatient Unit (they admitted me on 2/24). I know you’re talking with Dr. Whitman, but I also need you to hear some of this directly from me.

I didn’t tell you the full story before. On the night of 2/18, when I was staying at Elena’s apartment in Beaverton, I sat on her futon after everyone went to bed and wrote individual goodbye letters to my parents (Miguel and Rosa), to Daniel, and to the kids. I put them in a labeled folder in the bedroom closet with a list of my bank accounts and a note about the $12,400 still left on that joint credit card with Daniel. My plan was to kill myself the next time Sofia and Mateo went to his place for their Thursday–Sunday visit so they wouldn’t be the ones to find me.

Then on 2/16, during that telehealth with Dr. Priya Nair at the CascadeCare Gresham clinic, I actually told her I’d checked the February parenting plan order from Clark County to see which weekend (3/6–3/8) Daniel has the kids so I could time an overdose for then. Saying it out loud didn’t even scare me at the time. That scares me now.

And I still hadn’t reached out for help sooner. I kept telling myself I’d “deal with it later” and suddenly I was up against these dates I’d quietly picked out for dying. Honestly, that’s why everything feels so urgent now.

When I’m out, they’re talking about stepping me down to a Partial Hospitalization Program at Providence in Gresham or Glisan. I’d like your thoughts on how to make that work around the kids’ schedule and my job.

Thank you for sticking with me through all this.

Laura

metadata.subject: Things I didn’t say before I was admitted

artifacts.content:
Hey Ana,

I’m writing from the psych unit computer (never thought I’d type that sentence). I’ve been at Providence Center for Mental Health since 2/24 after Elena dragged me to the ED.

You remember how stressed I’ve been about CascadeCare. On 2/20, I had that performance review with Karen Lewis at the downtown office. She gave me a formal written warning for missing 5 shifts in 3 weeks. HR basically said if I can’t get back to full-time hours over the next 30 days, they might terminate me. I’ve been spiraling about losing our only health insurance and how I’d support Sofia and Mateo.

The irony is I work for a health insurance company and kind of assumed my own plan would just quietly cover whatever in‑network behavioral health I needed. I honestly didn’t realize there’d be all this prior auth and “utilization review” drama, even for an in-network place like Providence. It’s weird feeling like both the employee and the patient at the same time.

They’re talking about stepping me down to a PHP at Providence Medical Center on NE Glisan, probably 5 days a week for a few weeks. I keep trying to figure out how that lines up with the kids’ school pickup and whether I can negotiate some kind of temporary reduced schedule with work instead of getting fired.

On top of that, my brain keeps replaying the 2/23 morning on the I‑84 overpass near 102nd, when I sat there around 7 a.m. for almost 40 minutes trying to work up the nerve to drive off. The only reason I turned around was because Sofia texted asking if I’d be at her Lincoln Elementary conference that night.

Anyway, that’s a lot. How are you? Tell me something normal from your life, please.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Life from the other side of the phone tree

artifacts.content:
Personal note – treatment timeline + money stuff

I need to map out all the times I’ve crashed so I don’t gaslight myself later.

Spring 2023 – out of work for 3 months after that depressive episode and the partial hospitalization in Gresham. Mom and Papá started covering $275/month toward my credit card so I wouldn’t default. In May 2023 I set up that payment plan with Chase.

January 12–26, 2025 – first big Providence stay on the adult inpatient unit at 4805 NE Glisan after the suicide attempt at the NE Portland apartment. That’s when Dr. Angela Kim started me on lithium plus sertraline. CPS did the welfare check back in October 2023 after Daniel’s blow‑up, and all of that is already in the Providence chart.

Now February 24, 2026 – back inpatient at Providence Center for Mental Health – Adult Inpatient Unit, with Dr. Sarah K. Whitman as attending and Michael Lawson and Dr. Emily Carter looped in. They’re already talking about stepping down to a 4‑week PHP at Providence Medical Center.

I have to stay in‑network this time. If I went out-of-network for another inpatient plus PHP, I’d probably blow up the Chase plan and maybe even lose the 97218 house with Sofia and Mateo. Honestly, I picked Providence partly because it was the easiest to get into quickly, not necessarily because I sat down and compared every “clinical fit” option in the network.

I also jumped at the idea of another higher level of care without really exploring if something like intensive outpatient might have been enough. I just wanted to rip the Band‑Aid off and get started instead of sitting at home overthinking it.

Remember: housing and stability for the kids first.

Elena: How are you doing tonight? Kids are finally in bed.

Laura: Tired. Group was heavy. We talked about “means restriction” and I had to admit I still think about that morning on I‑84 by the 102nd exit, sitting there for 40 minutes at 7 a.m. on 2/23.

Elena: I still get chills thinking about that. I’m glad Sofia texted you about the Lincoln Elementary conference.

Laura: Me too. Also, they keep going over my history to plan next steps. It’s strange hearing them list everything – the Spring 2023 Gresham PHP, the Jan 12–26, 2025 inpatient at Providence Medical Center when Dr. Angela Kim started lithium and sertraline after the attempt at the NE apartment, the CPS welfare check in Oct 2023 because of Daniel.

Elena: At least you don’t have to retell it all from scratch.

Laura: Exactly. That’s why I’m sticking with Providence. They already know about Daniel’s drinking and the Dec 5, 2024 family court hearing where the judge limited his overnights after that October 2024 night when Sofia called Mom from his place in Vancouver, saying he was yelling and slamming doors. The safety plan from that admission is already in their system.

Elena: Makes sense. Less rehashing.

Laura: Honestly, I also chose this unit because it was the one that could get me in fastest. I didn’t have the bandwidth to shop around.

Elena: You did the right thing. We just needed you safe.

Laura: Yeah. How’s Abuela? Has she had any more confusion like after her March 2022 stroke at Adventist?

Elena: She’s okay today, just tired.

Laura: Give her a hug from me. I miss you all.

Hi Dad,

I’m borrowing the unit computer for a bit. I wanted to explain a little more about what’s happening and say thank you.

They admitted me on 2/24 to the Providence Center for Mental Health – Adult Inpatient Unit. My attending psychiatrist is Dr. Sarah K. Whitman, and she’s working with my therapist, Michael Lawson, and my primary doctor, Dr. Emily J. Carter. They’re saying I’ll probably go to a Partial Hospitalization Program at Providence Medical Center on NE Glisan after this, maybe 3–4 weeks.

I know you and Mom are already paying $275 every month toward my credit card from when I was out of work for 3 months in Spring 2023 after that depressive episode and PHP in Gresham. In May 2023 we set up that payment plan with Chase, and I still feel guilty every time I see the statement.

That’s one reason I’m staying in‑network at Providence. If I went out‑of‑network for this inpatient stay plus another 4‑week PHP, I’d probably default on that plan and might have to move Sofia and Mateo out of the house on NE 62nd in 97218. I don’t want their lives uprooted again.

You should also know how bad it got. On the night of 2/21 at the Gresham apartment, I only slept about 3 hours in two days, then went into the garage around 11:30 p.m., drank half a bottle of cheap vodka I’d hidden, and took 6 extra‑strength Tylenol PM on top of my 150 mg sertraline. I woke up on the floor. I told myself it “wasn’t really an attempt,” but the doctors here disagree.

Thank you for watching the kids, for the Sunday dinners, for everything. I’m trying to get well so I can be the kind of mom they deserve.

Con cariño,
Laura

Journal – late night on the unit

Lights-out was an hour ago but I can’t sleep, so I’m scribbling this in the little notebook they let us keep.

It’s wild to think how many times I’ve been on the edge and then tried to convince myself it “wasn’t that bad.” On 2/20, after the performance review with Karen Lewis at the CascadeCare downtown office and the written warning for missing 5 shifts in 3 weeks, I told myself I’d just power through the next 30 days so I wouldn’t lose the job or our insurance. Two days later, on 2/21 around 11:30 p.m., I was on the garage floor in Gresham after half a bottle of vodka and 6 Tylenol PM on top of 150 mg sertraline.

The team here keeps reminding me that outpatient hasn’t been enough: weekly therapy with Michael Lawson for 6 months, the sertraline increase from 100 mg to 150 mg by Dr. Carter in mid‑February, extra telehealth check‑ins, staying with Elena in Beaverton for a few nights. Still, I was writing goodbye letters on 2/18, planning an overdose for the 3/6–3/8 weekend when Daniel has the kids per the Clark County parenting plan, and sitting on the I‑84/102nd overpass for 40 minutes on 2/23 until Sofia’s text about her Lincoln Elementary conference snapped me out of it.

Part of me keeps whispering that if this process drags on, I can “probably just manage at home a little longer” – cook, do school drop‑offs, smile on Zoom calls. But another part of me knows how quickly I slide into rehearsing where the ropes are in the garage and which overpass has the lowest traffic.

I need to remember this when I start minimizing again.

metadata.title: Don’t minimize this
metadata.location: Providence Center for Mental Health – Adult Inpatient Unit

artifacts.content:
From: laura.hernandez@example.com
To: elena.rodriguez@example.com
Date: February 2, 2026
Subject: What’s happening tomorrow

Elena,

I want to write this out so it’s clear, because my brain feels like mush. Tomorrow, February 3, they’re admitting me to Providence Portland Medical Center’s locked behavioral health unit under ASAM Level 4.0 – medically managed intensive inpatient. This came out of the employee assistance program referral after Dr. Karen Liu (the EAP psychiatrist through CascadeCare Health Services) documented in her notes that for about three weeks straight I was drinking a fifth of vodka nightly and talking about passive suicidal thoughts during my late remote shifts.

On top of that, Dr. Angela Moreno at Riverside Counseling has been pushing for a higher level of care too. She wrote that my panic attacks and alcohol use got much worse after the custody exchange on January 28, 2026, when Daniel told Sofia, right in front of me, that “Mom is going back to the psych ward again.” I basically dissociated in front of the kids. That’s part of why she’s also recommending ASAM Level 3.7 at Providence Willamette Falls starting the same date, February 3, 2026, as a medically monitored option if the 4.0 bed falls through.

I’m scared but also a little relieved. Can you please help coordinate with Mom and Dad about the kids if Daniel starts making things harder again?

Love,
Laura

From: laura.hernandez@example.com
To: a.moreno@riversidecounseling.org
Date: February 27, 2026
Subject: Follow-up re: step-down + custody

Hi Dr. Moreno,

I wanted to summarize what we discussed for discharge planning. The inpatient team is recommending a step-down from the 24-hour locked psychiatric unit into ASAM Level 3.5 – clinically managed high‑intensity residential services at the Providence St. Vincent residential program beginning March 1, 2026. As we talked about, this isn’t just clinical; it’s tied directly to the temporary custody order from Clark County Family Court. Daniel Ortiz has a temporary order limiting my unsupervised time with Sofia and Mateo until I complete at least 30 days at this level of care (the 30 days residential requirement is actually written into the order).

You also mentioned documenting my work-related stressors. I’m still employed at CascadeCare Health Services, working remote in the call center, and I’m asking to use my in-network benefits at Providence. I’m honestly terrified that if I went to an out-of-network psych facility, a manual prior auth or claims review might cross the desk of my old supervisor, Mark Ellison, who gave me a written warning in November 2024 for missing 6 full shifts due to panic attacks. Keeping treatment in-network at Providence (including the likely 3–4 week Partial Hospitalization Program at Providence Medical Center PHP after residential) feels safer in terms of privacy and not worsening my standing at work.

If you can include these details in your note, I think it will help with both the court and insurance.

Thank you,
Laura Hernandez

From: laura.hernandez@example.com
To: hr@cascadecarehealth.com
Cc: melissa.grant@cascadecarehealth.com
Date: March 25, 2026
Subject: Request for medical leave accommodation and PHP/IOP schedule

To HR and Melissa,

Following my recent inpatient stay, my psychiatrist and treatment team are recommending a structured step‑down. First, they’ve requested ASAM Level 2.5 – Partial Hospitalization Program (PHP) at Providence Behavioral Health Center in Gresham, 5 days per week over 6 weeks starting April 7, 2026. This is directly related to the issues noted in Melissa Grant’s March 15, 2026 written warning from the CascadeCare Health Services remote call center: multiple call‑outs and documentation that I sounded impaired on member calls while tapering off clonazepam at home after discharge.

After PHP, the plan is to transition to ASAM Level 2.1 – intensive outpatient program (IOP) at Providence Milwaukie Hospital’s evening track, 3 nights per week for 12 weeks starting May 20, 2026. This is focused on my alcohol use, which escalated back in January 2026 when I was caring for my Abuela, Carmen Rodriguez, during a delirium episode at her senior apartment in Gresham. I was secretly finishing about half a bottle of wine in my car between visiting her and picking up the kids, which I now recognize as extremely unsafe.

I’m requesting a temporary adjustment to my schedule and protected leave so I can attend PHP (M–F for 6 weeks) and then evening IOP (3 nights/week for 12 weeks) while maintaining my employment. I’m committed to stabilizing my health so I can return to consistent, reliable performance.

Sincerely,
Laura Marie Hernandez
Senior Customer Support Specialist
